data.title: Scout24 (XTRA:G24) Net Margin At 37.4% Tests Bearish Narratives After Q1 2026 Results
data.description:
Scout24 (XTRA:G24) has kicked off 2026 with first quarter revenue of €185.4 million and basic EPS of €0.97, alongside net income excluding extra items of €68.5 million, setting a clear marker for how the year is starting to shape up. Over the past year, the company has seen trailing twelve month revenue move from €588.8 million to €692.1 million and basic EPS climb from €2.22 to €3.61, with trailing net margin moving from 28.3% to 37.4%. Investors are watching how these Q1 numbers sit against that backdrop of higher profitability. With that context, the latest results keep the focus firmly on margins and how effectively Scout24 is turning its top line into bottom line earnings.

See our full analysis for Scout24.

Next, the numbers will be set against the most widely held stories about Scout24 to see which narratives line up with this earnings run rate and which ones start to look out of date.

49.7% earnings growth sets the tone

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 49.7% while trailing net profit came in at €258.5 million on €692.1 million of revenue.
  • Bulls point to this combination of 49.7% earnings growth and a 37.4% net margin as backing their view that AI tools and higher tier subscriptions can keep compounding profits, yet
    • the same data also shows five year earnings growth averaging 19.8% per year, so the latest jump is much stronger than the longer run pace that optimistic investors refer to,
    • and forecasts for about 10.7% annual earnings growth are lower than the recent 49.7% figure. This means the bullish case relies on Q1 2026 strength being closer to the new normal than the more modest projections suggest.

Stronger recent growth and high margins are exactly what bullish investors like to see. This is where they argue the dedicated narrative really matters for understanding the upside they are focused on 🐂 Scout24 Bull Case

Margins at 37.4% challenge the bears

  • Trailing net profit margin stands at 37.4%, compared with 28.3% a year earlier, on a trailing revenue base that moved from €588.8 million to €692.1 million.
  • Bears worry that dependence on Germany and regulatory pressure could squeeze margins over time, yet
    • the reported 37.4% margin is already above the 35.9% starting point used in both the cautious and consensus forecasts, which assume future increases rather than compression,
    • and the Q1 2026 net income excluding extra items of €68.5 million on €185.4 million of revenue sits against this higher trailing margin backdrop, which does not yet reflect the earnings pressure that sceptics highlight.

For anyone weighing those concerns, the latest profitability figures give a concrete reference point before leaning too much into the more cautious narrative 🐻 Scout24 Bear Case

  • Consensus narrative suggests ongoing revenue growth of about 10.6% per year and rising margins toward 38.3%. Investors weighing this against current pricing can see that
    • the €71.00 share price sits below both the DCF fair value of €139.76 and the €103.69 analyst target, which is one reason some investors frame Scout24 as priced below modelled value even with a 19.3x P/E,
    • while that 19.3x P/E is higher than the peer average shown in the dataset but slightly below the 20.4x Global Interactive Media & Services industry figure, so relative valuation signals are mixed even when absolute value models suggest a gap to fair value.

data.title: Earnings Beat: Mabuchi Motor Co., Ltd. Just Beat Analyst Forecasts, And Analysts Have Been Updating Their Models
data.description:
Mabuchi Motor Co., Ltd. (TSE:6592) just released its latest quarterly results and things are looking bullish. The company beat forecasts, with revenue of JP¥50b, some 3.5% above estimates, and statutory earnings per share (EPS) coming in at JP¥23.62, 24% ahead of expectations. Following the result, the analysts have updated their earnings model, and it would be good to know whether they think there's been a strong change in the company's prospects, or if it's business as usual. So we collected the latest post-earnings statutory consensus estimates to see what could be in store for next year.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Taking into account the latest results, the consensus forecast from Mabuchi Motor's ten analysts is for revenues of JP¥209.2b in 2026. This reflects a modest 2.6% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are expected to plummet 29% to JP¥83.36 in the same period. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of JP¥208.3b and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥82.95 in 2026. The consensus analysts don't seem to have seen anything in these results that would have changed their view on the business, given there's been no major change to their estimates.

See our latest analysis for Mabuchi Motor

The analysts reconfirmed their price target of JP¥1,526, showing that the business is executing well and in line with expectations. It could also be instructive to look at the range of analyst estimates, to evaluate how different the outlier opinions are from the mean. Currently, the most bullish analyst values Mabuchi Motor at JP¥1,900 per share, while the most bearish prices it at JP¥1,100. These price targets show that analysts do have some differing views on the business, but the estimates do not vary enough to suggest to us that some are betting on wild success or utter failure.

One way to get more context on these forecasts is to look at how they compare to both past performance, and how other companies in the same industry are performing. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that Mabuchi Motor's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2026 expected to display 3.5% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 10% over the past five years. By way of comparison, the other companies in this industry with analyst coverage are forecast to grow their revenue at 6.9% per year. Factoring in the forecast slowdown in growth, it seems obvious that Mabuchi Motor is also expected to grow slower than other industry participants.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The most important thing to take away is that there's been no major change in sentiment, with the analysts reconfirming that the business is performing in line with their previous earnings per share estimates. On the plus side, there were no major changes to revenue estimates; although forecasts imply they will perform worse than the wider industry. The consensus price target held steady at JP¥1,526, with the latest estimates not enough to have an impact on their price targets.

With that said, the long-term trajectory of the company's earnings is a lot more important than next year. We have estimates - from multiple Mabuchi Motor analysts - going out to 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

Plus, you should also learn about the 2 warning signs we've spotted with Mabuchi Motor (including 1 which is a bit unpleasant) .

data.title: Central Japan Railway Company Just Recorded A 5.9% EPS Beat: Here's What Analysts Are Forecasting Next
data.description:
Central Japan Railway Company (TSE:9022) shareholders are probably feeling a little disappointed, since its shares fell 7.3% to JP¥3,744 in the week after its latest annual results. Central Japan Railway reported JP¥2.0t in revenue, roughly in line with analyst forecasts, although statutory earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥571 beat expectations, being 5.9% higher than what the analysts expected. Following the result, the analysts have updated their earnings model, and it would be good to know whether they think there's been a strong change in the company's prospects, or if it's business as usual. Readers will be glad to know we've aggregated the latest statutory forecasts to see whether the analysts have changed their mind on Central Japan Railway after the latest results.

Taking into account the latest results, Central Japan Railway's eleven analysts currently expect revenues in 2027 to be JP¥2.01t, approximately in line with the last 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are forecast to shrink 10.0% to JP¥521 in the same period. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of JP¥2.00t and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥525 in 2027. The consensus analysts don't seem to have seen anything in these results that would have changed their view on the business, given there's been no major change to their estimates.

View our latest analysis for Central Japan Railway

It will come as no surprise then, to learn that the consensus price target is largely unchanged at JP¥4,593. There's another way to think about price targets though, and that's to look at the range of price targets put forward by analysts, because a wide range of estimates could suggest a diverse view on possible outcomes for the business. There are some variant perceptions on Central Japan Railway, with the most bullish analyst valuing it at JP¥5,500 and the most bearish at JP¥3,350 per share. There are definitely some different views on the stock, but the range of estimates is not wide enough as to imply that the situation is unforecastable, in our view.

These estimates are interesting, but it can be useful to paint some more broad strokes when seeing how forecasts compare, both to the Central Japan Railway's past performance and to peers in the same industry. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that Central Japan Railway's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2027 expected to display 0.3% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 18% over the past five years. By way of comparison, the other companies in this industry with analyst coverage are forecast to grow their revenue at 2.5% per year. So it's pretty clear that, while revenue growth is expected to slow down, the wider industry is also expected to grow faster than Central Japan Railway.

The Bottom Line

The most obvious conclusion is that there's been no major change in the business' prospects in recent times, with the analysts holding their earnings forecasts steady, in line with previous estimates. On the plus side, there were no major changes to revenue estimates; although forecasts imply they will perform worse than the wider industry. There was no real change to the consensus price target, suggesting that the intrinsic value of the business has not undergone any major changes with the latest estimates.

data.title: GM’s C$691 Million V8 Bet Tests Balance Between ICE And EV Plans
data.description:
• General Motors (NYSE:GM) is committing $691 million to its St. Catharines, Ontario propulsion plant.
  • The investment will fund new V 8 engine production for trucks and SUVs in North America.
  • This decision deepens GM’s manufacturing footprint in Canada while other facilities in the country have recently closed.

For investors watching NYSE:GM, this move comes as the shares trade around $76.62 and reflect a long term gain of 143.4% over three years. The company’s choice to add fresh capital to a Canadian internal combustion engine plant comes at a time when attention has often centered on its electrification efforts and U.S. operations.

GM’s St. Catharines commitment highlights how the company is trying to balance EV ambitions with continued demand for trucks and SUVs. For your watchlist, this development adds another data point to how GM is positioning its North American manufacturing base and managing the mix between newer EV programs and established ICE platforms.

This C$691 million commitment to St. Catharines sits alongside a mixed set of recent signals for General Motors. On one hand, management is still allocating sizeable capital to internal combustion engine capacity in Canada, while also affirming a quarterly dividend of US$0.18 per share. On the other hand, GM has reported slightly lower Q1 2026 revenue of US$43,624 million versus US$44,020 million a year earlier and lower net income of US$2,627 million versus US$2,784 million, and has nudged full year 2026 net income guidance to a slightly lower range. For you as an investor, that combination points to a company committing cash to maintain its profitable truck and SUV base, even as EV plans are reworked and earnings expectations are reset. The St. Catharines news also comes against a backdrop of insider selling over the past year and no insider purchases, which may matter for those who weigh management alignment closely. Together, these threads suggest the engine investment is one piece in a broader capital allocation puzzle that includes dividends, plant spending, and ongoing guidance updates.

  • The St. Catharines expansion supports the narrative that GM is investing in North American manufacturing to support future earnings from trucks and SUVs, alongside its EV and software ambitions.
  • At the same time, slightly lower full year net income guidance and softer Q1 profit versus last year test the idea that heavy capital spending on manufacturing and EV programs will quickly translate into higher margins.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on tariffs, EV rollout, and software growth, while this Canadian plant investment and recent insider selling are less prominent, even though both can influence long term capital returns and perceived risk.

data.title: JTEKT (TSE:6473) Quarterly Loss And 0.6% Margin Challenge Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
JTEKT (TSE:6473) just posted its FY 2026 numbers with fourth quarter revenue of ¥521.6 billion and a basic EPS loss of ¥29.45, capping a year in which trailing twelve month revenue came in at ¥1.9 trillion and EPS was ¥37.62. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from ¥499.3 billion in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥454.8 billion in FY 2026 Q1, then to ¥475.4 billion in Q2 and ¥473.1 billion in Q3, alongside EPS shifting from ¥11.41 to ¥20.45, ¥17.76 and ¥28.86 respectively. This latest quarterly loss puts the spotlight firmly on how consistently the business can convert sales into profit.

See our full analysis for JTEKT.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to stack these earnings against the key market narratives around JTEKT to see which stories are backed up by the numbers and which ones are being questioned by the latest margin profile.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM profit only ¥11.97b on ¥1.92t sales

  • Over the last twelve months JTEKT generated ¥1.92t in revenue and ¥11.97b in net income, which works out to a net margin of about 0.6% compared with 0.7% a year earlier and five year earnings that have compounded at about a 3.6% decline per year.
  • What stands out for a bullish view that focuses on forecast earnings growth of 60.6% a year is that the current earnings base is small relative to sales. Recent quarterly profit swung from ¥9.19b in FY 2026 Q3 to a ¥9.38b loss in Q4, so investors who buy into that bullish story are effectively betting that margins on this large revenue base can improve meaningfully from a very low starting point.
    • Supporters of the bullish angle may point to the trailing twelve month EPS of ¥37.62 and argue that a move from a 0.6% margin closer to even low single digits would have a big impact on profit, but the data given still show margin pressure year on year.
    • Critics of that bullish narrative can highlight that the latest quarter produced a basic EPS loss of ¥29.45 and that earnings have declined on average over five years, so the historical record in the numbers does not yet reflect the sharp improvement implied by the 60.6% earnings growth forecast.

High P/E and dividend cover questions

  • JTEKT trades on a P/E of 50.9x versus a peer average of 14.3x and a Japan auto components industry average of 9.7x, while the dividend yield of 3.65% is flagged as not well covered by either earnings or free cash flow.
  • Bears argue that paying a 50.9x earnings multiple for a company with a 0.6% trailing net margin and a dividend that is not covered by earnings or cash flow leaves little room for error, and the data here back up that concern by showing modest margins and a history of earnings decline even as the dividend is being paid out.
    • The combination of weak coverage of a 3.65% yield and a high earnings multiple compared with peers is exactly the type of profile critics point to when they warn about valuation risk in income oriented stocks.
    • On top of this, revenue is only forecast to grow 1.6% per year compared with a 5.9% baseline for the Japan market, which means bears can argue that the premium P/E is not obviously supported by faster top line growth in the figures provided.

Large gap to DCF fair value

  • The current share price of ¥1,913 sits well below the stated DCF fair value estimate of ¥5,154.59, a gap that presents a very large difference between market price and that modelled valuation.
  • Supporters testing a bullish narrative around valuation will note that this large gap, combined with an earnings growth forecast of 60.6% a year, heavily supports the idea that the stock could be mispriced relative to its cash flow potential, even though the same data also point to risks such as a 0.6% net margin and a dividend that is not covered.
    • One side of the debate is that a share price far below a DCF fair value can reflect caution around the modest 1.6% revenue growth forecast and the recent quarterly loss, rather than an outright rejection of the model.
    • The opposing view is that, if the 60.6% earnings growth forecast is achieved while revenue trends stay roughly in line with the 1.92t trailing figure, then the current margin profile could look very different from the 0.6% level that is feeding into today’s P/E and dividend coverage concerns.

data.title: Apple Regulatory Fights Test Durability Of Services Margins And Ecosystem
data.description:
• Apple (NasdaqGS:AAPL) is contesting tighter oversight of its App Store as a high profile U.S. payments case moves toward the Supreme Court.
  • At the same time, Apple is challenging India’s competition authority, arguing that an antitrust probe and related disclosure demands go beyond its mandate.
  • These disputes focus on external payment links for apps in the U.S. and financial data access in India, both tied directly to Apple’s services and platform policies.

For investors following Apple primarily for its hardware franchise and quarterly numbers, these legal clashes are closely connected to its services ambitions. The App Store and broader digital ecosystem sit alongside iPhone, Mac, and wearables as key pillars of the NasdaqGS:AAPL narrative, particularly as regulators increase scrutiny of large tech platforms.

As these cases progress, attention may remain on how much flexibility Apple retains around fees, payment flows, and data sharing in major markets. For shareholders, the emphasis is less on any single ruling and more on the cumulative effect of tighter rules on Apple’s longer term approach to monetizing its platform.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Apple by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Apple.

Is Apple's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

For Apple, the regulatory disputes in the U.S. and India go straight to how it earns money from its ecosystem rather than just headline legal risk. The U.S. case keeps external payment links and reduced App Store fees in play as the matter heads toward the Supreme Court. That keeps some pressure on high margin services revenue, especially compared with platform peers like Alphabet and Microsoft that also face scrutiny on app distribution and search payments. In India, Apple’s pushback against the competition authority’s data requests highlights how far regulators are willing to go on financial disclosure and app market access in a large growth market. Investors tracking Apple’s services mix and regional exposure will likely treat these cases as part of a wider pattern, not isolated disputes.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Apple Narrative

  • The U.S. and Indian proceedings line up with the narrative theme that regulatory pressure around the App Store and services is a key factor for Apple’s long term earnings resilience.
  • Legal challenges to Apple’s fees, data practices, and financial disclosure could work against the assumption that services stay relatively stable and high margin compared with hardware.
  • The India case’s focus on detailed financial data access and the evolving rules on external payment links may not be fully captured in the narrative’s broad reference to regulatory headwinds.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Apple to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ A stricter App Store regime in the U.S., including sustained external payment links and limits on fees, could reduce services profitability and narrow Apple’s pricing flexibility relative to peers like Alphabet and Amazon.
  • ⚠️ An assertive competition authority in India could lead to wider remedies, such as changes in app distribution rules or additional disclosure, which would raise compliance costs in a market Apple has been expanding into.
  • 🎁 Apple is actively contesting the scope of oversight, which may give investors more clarity over time on how far regulators can go on fees, data access, and platform design.
  • 🎁 Tim Cook’s shift to executive chairman with a brief for policymaker engagement, alongside incoming CEO John Ternus, may help Apple coordinate its legal and regulatory approach while keeping the product roadmap in focus.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on any interim orders in the U.S. case that change App Store payment rules, as well as signals from the Supreme Court on if and how it will hear Apple’s appeal. In India, watch for court decisions on the competition authority’s information requests and any move to expand remedies beyond this specific investigation. Earnings calls and filings are also worth tracking for new disclosures on services fee structures, regional regulatory risks, and how management describes the balance between user privacy, data sharing, and compliance.

data.title: Capital Power Extends Arlington Valley Deal As New CFO Takes Over
data.description:
• Capital Power extended its Arlington Valley tolling agreement through 2038, increasing contracted capacity by 35 MW.
  • The Arlington Valley facility now has greater long term revenue visibility tied to peak seasonal electricity demand.
  • Kevin MacIntosh has been appointed as Capital Power's new Chief Financial Officer.

Capital Power, traded on the TSX under the ticker TSX:CPX, is drawing attention with these contract and leadership updates. The shares recently closed at CA$62.37, with a 1 year return of 24.6% and a 5 year return of 104.4%. This provides important context for assessing what these developments may mean for investors following the company.

The extension of Arlington Valley's contract, along with a 35 MW capacity increase, highlights a closer alignment between the asset and peak demand periods. The CFO transition to Kevin MacIntosh is occurring at the same time, so readers may want to observe how capital allocation, financing choices, and risk management evolve as the company continues its commercial optimization and portfolio diversification efforts.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Capital Power by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Capital Power.

2 things going right for Capital Power that this headline doesn't cover.

The Arlington Valley tolling extension through 2038 ties a larger slice of Capital Power’s US gas capacity to long term, contract-backed payments, which can matter for a business that also has exposure to merchant power markets. With roughly 75% of cash flow described as secured through contracts or hedges, this deal fits the company’s focus on visibility. For you as an investor, that can make it easier to think about how contracted earnings stack up against the company’s dividend commitments and interest costs.

How This Fits Into The Capital Power Narrative

  • The longer-dated Arlington Valley contract supports the narrative that recontracting existing assets and securing long term agreements can help underpin cash flow and support growth plans.
  • The sharp year on year fall in reported Q1 net income to CA$15 million, despite higher sales, highlights the concern in the narrative that margins may be vulnerable if costs, regulation, or competition move against the company.
  • The arrival of a new CFO, after analysts updated their views on margins and earnings power, introduces a leadership element that the existing narrative around execution and capital allocation does not fully address.

  • ⚠️ The common share dividend and preference share dividends are not well covered by earnings or free cash flow, which raises questions about how payout levels might interact with future investment needs.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow at a double digit rate, which, if achieved, could help ease current pressure points around interest cover and dividend sustainability.
  • 🎁 The Arlington Valley extension adds contracted revenue on top of a diversified portfolio across geographies and technologies, which can help reduce reliance on any single power market or asset.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how the new CFO approaches capital allocation, debt management, and hedging, especially after a quarter where net income moved sharply compared to the prior year. The interaction between long term contracted cash flows, interest costs, and dividend policy will likely remain a key focus, along with how future contract renewals compare to current terms in markets where Capital Power competes with peers such as TransAlta, Emera, or Brookfield Renewable. data.title: Galaxy Digital (TSX:GLXY) Q4 Loss Of US$1.04 EPS Tests Bullish Profitability Thesis
data.description:
Galaxy Digital (TSX:GLXY) has just posted another mixed set of numbers, with Q4 2025 revenue of about US$10.2b and a basic EPS loss of US$1.04 per share, rounding out a trailing twelve month period where revenue came in at roughly US$61.4b and basic EPS was a loss of US$0.53. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move between about US$8.7b and US$29.2b while basic EPS has swung from a profit of US$3.83 per share in late 2024 to alternating gains and losses through 2025. Investors are likely to focus on how this volatility in earnings power feeds into the margin story now.

See our full analysis for Galaxy Digital.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this pattern of heavy revenue and uneven profitability lines up with the dominant bullish and bearish narratives around Galaxy Digital.

See what the community is saying about Galaxy Digital

Advertisement

Revenue swings from $8.7b to $29.2b in recent quarters

  • Over the last six reported quarters, total revenue has ranged from about US$8.7b in Q3 2024 to US$29.2b in Q3 2025, with Q4 2025 at US$10.2b. This shows that the top line has been large but quite variable.
  • Bulls argue that as Helios and other infrastructure assets move from build out to operation, revenue could become more tied to recurring, long duration cash flows. However, the wide revenue band in the recent history means investors are still waiting for the steadier pattern that the bullish view expects.
    • The bullish narrative points to Helios and tokenization tooling as sources of more fee based revenue. At the same time, the trailing twelve month revenue of about US$61.4b is still coming through with a loss of US$84.9m, so that shift has not yet shown up in the bottom line.
    • Bulls also highlight growing institutional engagement and record client onboarding, but the alternating revenue levels from roughly US$8.7b to US$29.2b suggest that trading and balance sheet activity are still major drivers of results.
Bulls say this is the setup for a powerful earnings reset as Helios ramps, yet the current loss making profile means that thesis is still unproven for now, so it can be worth reading the full bull case before deciding how much weight to give it. 🐂 Galaxy Digital Bull Case

Unprofitable today with widening losses over five years

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Galaxy Digital reported a net income loss of US$84.9m and a basic EPS loss of US$0.53 per share, and over the past five years losses have widened by about 12% per year according to the analysis data.
  • Bears focus on this persistent loss making record and argue that even with forecast earnings growth of about 66.61% per year and expectations of profitability within three years, the path from a US$84.9m loss to positive earnings still depends heavily on execution in areas like Helios, tokenization and venture realizations.
    • Critics highlight that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, so while losses have averaged about a 12% annual increase, there is less room for error if new projects underperform or if write downs in illiquid holdings continue.
    • The share price has also been described as volatile over the past three months, so the combination of current unprofitability and balance sheet pressure is central to the bearish narrative when questioning how smooth that turnaround can be.
Skeptics point to that widening loss trend and financing pressure as reasons to study the full cautious case on Galaxy before getting comfortable with the risk profile. 🐻 Galaxy Digital Bear Case

Low 0.1x P/S versus peers despite growth forecasts

  • Galaxy Digital trades on a P/S of 0.1x versus a peer average of 2.6x and a US Capital Markets industry average of 3.6x, while analysts are modeling revenue growth of about 27.1% per year and earnings growth of roughly 66.61% per year, with a move to profitability expected within three years.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that the analyst price target of US$39.97 is well above the current share price of US$25.42. That view has to be balanced against the fact that the business is still loss making and that the same consensus expects margins only to move from roughly negative to around breakeven in a few years.
    • What stands out is that the price target implies meaningful upside from today while the most recent trailing twelve month figures still show a net loss of US$84.9m, so the gap between current profitability and future expectations is wide.
    • At the same time, the low P/S can be seen as the market demanding proof that the modeled 27.1% revenue growth and return to profitability within three years actually come through in reported numbers.
